events.title: Byblos Established as Major Port City
events.detail: Byblos (ancient Gebal), on the Lebanese coast north of Beirut, emerges as one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities. Its harbour traded cedar wood, papyrus, and resin with Egypt — the Greek name 'Byblos' (from which 'Bible' derives) reflects the papyrus trade. Archaeological layers reveal occupation from at least 5000 BCE, with monumental urban features from c.2700 BCE.

events.title: Phoenician City-States Flourish — Tyre, Sidon, Byblos
events.detail: After the Bronze Age collapse (~1200 BCE), Phoenician city-states of Tyre, Sidon, and Byblos rise as the dominant maritime trading powers of the eastern Mediterranean. Tyre under King Hiram I (~969–936 BCE) forms alliance with Solomon of Israel, supplying cedar and craftsmen for the First Temple. The Phoenicians develop a 22-consonant alphabet — the ancestor of all Western alphabets — by approximately 1050 BCE.

sources.name: Oxford Handbook of the Phoenician and Punic Mediterranean

events.title: Carthage Founded — Phoenician Colonisation Peak
events.detail: Phoenicians from Tyre, led by the legendary Queen Dido (Elissa), establish Carthage (Qart-Hadasht — 'New City') on the North African coast near modern Tunis. The Phoenician colonial network extends from Cyprus to Spain and the Atlantic coast, with over 300 settlements. Arwad, Sidon, and Tyre maintain competing trade empires across the Mediterranean.

events.title: Alexander the Great Besieges and Destroys Tyre
events.detail: Alexander of Macedon launches a seven-month siege of island Tyre (Jan–Aug 332 BCE) — the most famous military engineering feat of antiquity. He builds a kilometre-long mole (causeway) from the mainland to the island, breaking the city's naval defences. Tyre falls in August 332 BCE; Alexander kills 8,000 defenders and sells 30,000 into slavery. This conquest ends Phoenician independence. The mole silted up, turning Tyre into a peninsula connected to the mainland.

events.title: Pompey Integrates Phoenicia into Roman Syria
events.detail: Roman general Pompey annexes the Seleucid remnants in 64 BCE, incorporating Phoenicia into the province of Syria. The coastal cities prosper under Pax Romana; Berytus (Beirut) is refounded as a Roman colony under Augustus. Berytus becomes famous throughout the empire for its prestigious law school (Schola Legum Berytensis), producing jurists whose works were compiled into the Corpus Juris Civilis under Justinian.

events.title: Temple Complex of Baalbek (Heliopolis) Constructed
events.detail: The Romans build the largest religious complex in the empire at Heliopolis (Baalbek) in the Bekaa Valley. Construction of the Temple of Jupiter begins under Augustus (~27 BCE) and continues for over two centuries. The Temple of Bacchus (~150 CE) follows. The site uses monolithic stones weighing up to 800 tonnes — the largest cut stones ever moved by humans in antiquity. Today Baalbek is a UNESCO World Heritage Site that also hosts Hezbollah's stronghold and Iran-funded Baalbek International Festival.

events.title: Maronite Christian Community Established
events.detail: Followers of the hermit monk Saint Maron (d. ~410 CE) in the Orontes Valley form what becomes the Maronite Church. After the Council of Chalcedon (451 CE), Maronites align with Rome against Monophysite movements. They gradually migrate into the mountains of Mount Lebanon, which become their primary refuge under Arab rule. The Maronite Church remains the most defining cultural institution of Lebanese national identity, with the presidency reserved for Maronite Christians under the National Pact.

sources.name: Dib, Pierre — History of the Maronite Church (1971)

events.title: Arab Muslim Armies Conquer Lebanon
events.detail: Following the Battle of Yarmouk (Aug 636 CE), Arab forces under the Rashidun Caliphate sweep through the Levant. The coastal cities of Tyre, Sidon, Beirut, and Byblos fall to Muslim armies by 638–641 CE. Maronite communities retreat further into Mount Lebanon. Arabic gradually replaces Greek and Aramaic, though mountain communities maintain Syriac liturgical traditions. The Umayyad Caliphate (661–750) centres its empire at Damascus, with Lebanon as a frontier province.

sources.name: al-Baladhuri, Futuh al-Buldan (Conquests of the Lands)
Kennedy, Hugh — The Great Arab Conquests (2007)

events.title: Crusader County of Tripoli Established
events.detail: Raymond IV of Toulouse initiates the siege of Tripoli; his cousin Bertrand completes the conquest and establishes the County of Tripoli in 1109 — the last Crusader state founded. The county, stretching from Tripoli south toward Beirut, exists until 1289 when Mamluks under Sultan Qalawun destroy Tripoli. The Crusader castle at Beaufort (Qal'at al-Shaqif) and the Castle of St Louis at Sidon stand as architectural remnants still visible today.

events.title: Fakhr al-Din II Creates Autonomous Lebanese Emirate
events.detail: Fakhr al-Din II Ma'ni (r. 1590–1635), Druze emir of the Chouf, expands his authority over most of present-day Lebanon and parts of Syria and Palestine, creating the most autonomous Arab entity within the Ottoman Empire. He allies with Ferdinand I de' Medici of Tuscany, introduces European agricultural and architectural practices, and attracts both Maronite and Druze loyalty. Ottomans defeat and execute him in 1635, but his legacy shapes the concept of a distinct Lebanese polity separate from Syria.

sources.name: Salibi, Kamal — A House of Many Mansions: The History of Lebanon Reconsidered (1988)

events.title: Druze-Maronite Civil War — 11,000 Killed, France Intervenes
events.detail: Intercommunal tensions between Druze and Maronite communities explode in May–July 1860. Druze forces kill an estimated 11,000 Maronites across Mount Lebanon; the violence spreads to Damascus where thousands of Christians are massacred. France dispatches 6,000 troops (Napoleon III) to restore order — the first Western military intervention in Lebanon's history. The violence accelerates European pressure on the Ottoman Empire for administrative reform of the region.

sources.name: Fawaz, Leila Tarazi — An Occasion for War: Civil Conflict in Lebanon and Damascus in 1860 (1994)

events.title: Mount Lebanon Mutasarrifate — Autonomous Province Established
events.detail: The Great Powers (France, Britain, Russia, Austria, Prussia) pressure the Ottomans to grant Mount Lebanon special autonomous status. The Règlement Organique creates the Mutasarrifate of Mount Lebanon — governed by a non-Lebanese Ottoman Christian with a 12-member administrative council representing the six sects. The arrangement lasts until 1915. The system's confessional power-sharing becomes the model for Lebanon's future political structure.

sources.name: Traboulsi, Fawwaz — A History of Modern Lebanon (2007)

events.title: Great Famine of Mount Lebanon — 200,000 Dead
events.detail: During World War I, the Ottoman authorities blockade Mount Lebanon to punish suspected French sympathisers, compounded by Allied naval blockades. A locust plague destroys crops in 1915. By 1918, an estimated 200,000 people — approximately one-third of Mount Lebanon's population — have died of starvation, disease, and exposure. The famine creates profound trauma that fuels Lebanese nationalist sentiment and demand for French protection after the war.

sources.name: Schilcher, Linda Schatkowski — The Famine of 1915-1918 in Greater Syria (1992)

events.title: France Proclaims Greater Lebanon
events.detail: On September 1, 1920, French General Henri Gouraud proclaims 'Le Grand Liban' from the Baabda Palace, incorporating Mount Lebanon with Beirut, the Bekaa Valley, and Southern Lebanon — territories previously part of Ottoman Syria. The borders roughly follow those of modern Lebanon. Arab nationalists who sought a unified Arab state protest vigorously. The Maronite Church welcomes the new entity. The modern Lebanese state is born — but its sectarian and territorial tensions are baked in from the start.

sources.name: Traboulsi, Fawwaz — A History of Modern Lebanon (2007)

events.title: National Pact — Lebanese Independence Achieved
events.detail: On November 22, 1943 — observed as Independence Day — Lebanon achieves independence from French Mandate authority. The National Pact (al-Mithaq al-Watani), an unwritten agreement between President Bishara al-Khoury (Maronite) and Prime Minister Riad el-Solh (Sunni), codifies the confessional formula: the president is Maronite, PM is Sunni, and parliamentary speaker is Shia. Parliamentary seats are distributed 6:5 Christian-to-Muslim. The pact becomes Lebanon's foundational political contract.

events.title: Nakba — 100,000+ Palestinian Refugees Enter Lebanon
events.detail: The 1948 Arab-Israeli War and the Nakba ('catastrophe') drive approximately 100,000–130,000 Palestinians into Lebanon, settling mainly in 12 UNRWA refugee camps. The refugees are overwhelmingly Sunni Muslim, altering Lebanon's carefully calibrated confessional balance. Lebanon refuses to naturalise Palestinian refugees, citing demographic concerns. The camps in Sabra, Shatila, Ain el-Hilweh, and Nahr el-Bared become permanent settlements lasting to the present day.

sources.name: Morris, Benny — The Birth of the Palestinian Refugee Problem Revisited (2004)

events.title: Cairo Agreement — Lebanon Grants PLO Autonomy in South
events.detail: Under Egyptian mediation, Lebanon and the PLO sign the Cairo Agreement (November 3, 1969), granting the Palestine Liberation Organisation the right to operate militarily from Lebanese territory, particularly in the South and refugee camps. The agreement gives the PLO de facto sovereignty within the camps. Critics call it a 'state within a state' arrangement. PLO operations from Lebanon trigger Israeli reprisals against Lebanese civilians, fuelling domestic resentment that contributes to the 1975 civil war.

sources.name: Traboulsi, Fawwaz — A History of Modern Lebanon (2007)

events.title: Ain el-Rummaneh Bus Massacre — Civil War Begins
events.detail: On April 13, 1975, Phalangist militia members ambush a bus carrying Palestinian and Lebanese Nasserist passengers through the Christian neighbourhood of Ain el-Rummaneh, killing 27. The attack — a retaliation for an earlier assassination attempt on Phalange leader Pierre Gemayel — ignites Lebanon's civil war. Within weeks fighting spreads across Beirut along the 'Green Line.' The war ultimately kills over 100,000 people, displaces one million, and devastates the Lebanese economy over 15 years.

sources.name: Hanf, Theodor — Coexistence in Wartime Lebanon (1993)

events.title: Tel al-Zaatar Siege — 2,000–3,500 Palestinians Killed
events.detail: From January to August 1976, Maronite militias (Kataeb Regulatory Forces, National Liberal Party Tigers) besiege the Palestinian refugee camp of Tel al-Zaatar in East Beirut. Syria intervenes militarily in June 1976 to constrain PLO power. The camp falls on August 12, 1976; between 2,000 and 3,500 Palestinian civilians and fighters are killed during the siege and its immediate aftermath. The massacre deepens Lebanon's civil war and entrenches Palestinian-Maronite enmity.

sources.name: Khalidi, Rashid — Under Siege: PLO Decisionmaking During the 1982 War (1986)

events.title: Operation Litani — First Israeli Invasion, UNIFIL Established
events.detail: On March 14, 1978, following a PLO coastal road attack in Israel killing 38 Israelis, the IDF invades Southern Lebanon up to the Litani River with 25,000 troops. The operation kills approximately 1,100–2,000 Lebanese and Palestinians, displaces 250,000. Israel hands territory to the South Lebanon Army (Major Sa'd Haddad). The UN Security Council passes Resolution 425, establishing UNIFIL (United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon) to monitor Israeli withdrawal and restore peace.

sources.name: Hiro, Dilip — Lebanon: Fire and Embers (1993)

events.title: Operation Peace for Galilee — Israel Reaches Beirut
events.detail: On June 6, 1982, Israeli forces under Ariel Sharon invade Lebanon with 76,000 troops following the assassination attempt on Ambassador Shlomo Argov. Forces reach and besiege West Beirut for 10 weeks, forcing PLO evacuation (Aug–Sep 1982). The Lebanese civilian death toll is estimated at 17,000–20,000. IDF occupies South Lebanon and maintains a 'Security Zone' until 2000. The invasion leads to the creation of Hezbollah and triggers the US Marine and French peacekeeping deployment.

sources.name: Schiff, Ze'ev and Ya'ari, Ehud — Israel's Lebanon War (1984)

events.title: Sabra and Shatila Massacre — 800–3,500 Killed
events.detail: From September 16–18, 1982, Lebanese Phalangist militia fighters enter the Palestinian refugee camps of Sabra and Shatila in West Beirut — with Israeli forces controlling the perimeter and illuminating the camps at night with flares — and massacre between 800 and 3,500 Palestinian civilians and Lebanese Shia residents. The killings follow the assassination of President-elect Bashir Gemayel. Israel's Kahan Commission finds Ariel Sharon bears 'personal responsibility.' The UN condemns the killings as an act of genocide (Res. 37/123D).

events.title: US Marine Barracks Bombing — 241 Americans Killed
events.detail: On October 23, 1983, two simultaneous suicide truck bombings hit the US Marine barracks and the French paratroopers' headquarters in Beirut Airport complex. The attack on the Marines kills 241 US service members — the deadliest single-day loss for the US military since Iwo Jima. Simultaneously, 58 French paratroopers are killed. Islamic Jihad (widely understood to be Hezbollah's predecessor) claims responsibility. The bombings lead to US and French military withdrawal from Lebanon by early 1984.

sources.name: US Department of Defense Commission on Beirut Bombing (1983)

events.title: Hezbollah Publishes Open Letter — Formal Emergence
events.detail: Hezbollah (Party of God) emerges from Shia Amal splinters and Iranian Revolutionary Guards operatives following the 1982 Israeli invasion. On February 16, 1985, the organisation publishes its founding Open Letter calling for an Islamic republic in Lebanon and the elimination of Israel. Iran provides funding, training, and ideological guidance through the IRGC-Quds Force. Hezbollah simultaneously runs social services in Shia communities, building a parallel state infrastructure — welfare, hospitals, schools — that proves more enduring than its military manifesto.

sources.name: Hezbollah Open Letter (1985) — published in Lebanese press
Norton, Augustus Richard — Hezbollah: A Short History (2007)

events.title: Taif Accord — Arab League Ends the Civil War
events.detail: Under Saudi, Moroccan, and Algerian mediation, Lebanese parliamentarians meet in Taif, Saudi Arabia and sign the Document of National Accord (October 22, 1989). The accord redistributes power from the Maronite president to the Council of Ministers; formalises Syria's role as guarantor; calls for disbanding militias; and mandates electoral reform. Fighting continues until Syria crushes Michel Aoun in October 1990, ending the formal civil war. The Taif system remains Lebanon's constitutional framework today, though Hezbollah uniquely retained its arms.

sources.name: Zisser, Eyal — Lebanon: The Challenge of Independence (2000)

events.title: Operation Accountability — Israel Bombs South Lebanon
events.detail: In July 1993, Israel launches 'Operation Accountability' — seven days of intense bombardment of South Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley — to pressure the Lebanese government to restrain Hezbollah rocket attacks on northern Israel. Over 300,000 Lebanese civilians are displaced; 140+ killed. The operation fails to eliminate Hezbollah and instead generates popular sympathy for the group. An informal 'understanding' reached through US mediation limits but does not end cross-border violence.

sources.name: HRW – Civilian Pawns: Laws of War Violations in Lebanon (1994)

events.title: Qana Massacre — 106 Refugees Killed at UN Compound
events.detail: On April 18, 1996, during Israel's 'Operation Grapes of Wrath,' Israeli artillery shells a UNIFIL compound at Qana (Cana) where approximately 800 Lebanese civilians had taken shelter. The shelling kills 106 civilians, including many children. The UN investigation finds Israel's targeting was not an error but deliberate. The massacre causes worldwide outrage and US pressure to end the operation. Qana becomes a symbol of Lebanese civilian suffering from Israeli military operations.

sources.name: UN Secretary-General Report on Qana Incident (1996)
HRW – Israel's Conduct in South Lebanon (1996)

events.title: Israel Withdraws from South Lebanon After 22 Years
events.detail: On May 25, 2000, Israel completes a unilateral withdrawal from South Lebanon and the 'Security Zone,' ending a 22-year occupation. Hezbollah claims a decisive victory and celebrates 'Liberation Day' (now a national holiday). The rapid Israeli withdrawal — soldiers sometimes abandoning positions overnight — triggers the collapse of the South Lebanon Army (SLA); its members flee to Israel. UNIFIL verifies the withdrawal along the 'Blue Line.' The withdrawal fundamentally boosts Hezbollah's regional prestige and becomes its primary legitimising narrative.

events.title: PM Rafik Hariri Assassinated — Cedar Revolution Follows
events.detail: On February 14, 2005, former Prime Minister Rafik Hariri and 21 others are killed when a massive car bomb (1,800 kg of explosives) detonates near the St George Hotel on Beirut's Corniche. The assassination triggers the Cedar Revolution — mass protests demanding Syrian withdrawal. Syria withdraws its last troops from Lebanon on April 26, 2005 after 29 years. The UN Special Tribunal for Lebanon is established, and in 2020 convicts senior Hezbollah member Salim Ayyash in absentia.

sources.name: Special Tribunal for Lebanon – Ayyash et al. Judgment (2020)
L'Orient Le Jour – Hariri Assassination Coverage (2005)

events.title: Second Lebanon War — 34 Days, 1,200+ Lebanese Killed
events.detail: On July 12, 2006, Hezbollah kidnaps two Israeli soldiers in a cross-border raid and kills eight. Israel launches a 34-day war: intensive air campaign strikes 7,000 targets including Beirut's Dahiyeh suburb, bridges, roads, and the port; ground forces advance into South Lebanon. Hezbollah fires over 4,000 rockets into northern Israel. Lebanese death toll exceeds 1,191 (mostly civilians); 159 Israelis die. Israel destroys an estimated $3.6 billion in Lebanese infrastructure. UNSCR 1701 ends the fighting on August 14, expanding UNIFIL to 15,000.

sources.name: HRW – Why They Died: Civilian Casualties in Lebanon (2007)

events.title: Doha Agreement — After Hezbollah Seizes West Beirut
events.detail: In May 2008, the Lebanese government attempts to shut down Hezbollah's private telecommunications network. Hezbollah responds by seizing West Beirut militarily (May 7–9) — the most serious internal armed confrontation since the civil war. Qatar mediates the Doha Agreement (May 21, 2008): Hezbollah gains veto power in the cabinet ('blocking third'), Michel Suleiman becomes president ending an 18-month vacancy, and early elections are held. The agreement marks a turning point in Hezbollah's domestic political dominance.

sources.name: Doha Agreement (Agreement of National Understanding, May 21, 2008)
ICG Middle East Report N°87 – Lebanon: Hizbollah's Gamble (2008)

events.title: Hezbollah Publicly Enters Syrian Civil War at Qusayr
events.detail: In April–May 2013, Hezbollah openly commits combat forces to the Syrian civil war alongside Assad's army in the Battle of Qusayr. Secretary-General Nasrallah declares Hezbollah's involvement as existential necessity against takfiri groups. The intervention marks a major shift: Hezbollah transitions from a resistance movement against Israel to a regional sectarian power-projection force. The involvement exacerbates sectarian tensions in Lebanon, with Sunni jihadist reprisals including multiple Beirut bombings.

sources.name: BBC – Hezbollah Says It Is Fighting in Syria (May 2013)
ICG Middle East Report – Lebanon and the Syrian War

events.title: October 17 Revolution — Mass Protests Against Ruling Elite
events.detail: On October 17, 2019, a proposed WhatsApp tax triggers mass spontaneous protests that rapidly expand into the largest in Lebanon's modern history. Hundreds of thousands take to the streets across all 26 districts, demanding the resignation of the entire political class ('All of them means all of them' — 'Kullun ya'ni kullun'). Prime Minister Saad Hariri resigns October 29. The protests persist for months but fail to produce systemic change as the political elite resists reform, and the COVID-19 pandemic and economic collapse redirect national energy.

sources.name: L'Orient Le Jour – Thawra: One Year After (2020)

events.title: Lebanon Defaults on Sovereign Debt — Financial Collapse Begins
events.detail: On March 9, 2020, Lebanon defaults on its $1.2 billion Eurobond payment — the first sovereign default in the country's modern history. The Lebanese pound, pegged at 1,507 LBP/$ since 1997, collapses on the black market: 10,000/$1 by late 2020, 90,000/$1 by 2022-23. Banks freeze depositor accounts and impose informal capital controls. The IMF estimates Lebanon's financial losses at $72 billion. Over 80% of the population falls below the poverty line by 2022 — what the World Bank calls 'one of the top three most severe global financial crises since the mid-19th century.'

sources.name: World Bank – Lebanon Economic Monitor Spring 2021

events.title: Beirut Port Explosion — 217 Killed, 300,000 Displaced
events.detail: On August 4, 2020 at 18:07 local time, 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate stored unsafely in Warehouse 12 of the Port of Beirut detonates in one of the largest non-nuclear explosions in history. The blast kills 217 people, injures more than 7,000, destroys 77,000 buildings, and displaces 300,000 residents. The explosion is felt as far as Cyprus. Judge Tarek Bitar's investigation is repeatedly suspended following legal challenges by accused officials including former ministers. No senior official has been convicted as of 2026.

sources.name: HRW – They Killed Us from the Inside: An Investigation of the Aug 4 Beirut Blast (2021)
L'Orient Le Jour – Beirut Port Explosion

events.title: Hezbollah Opens 'Support Front' After Oct 7
events.detail: The day after Hamas's October 7, 2023 attacks on Israel, Hezbollah opens a cross-border exchange along the Lebanon-Israel border, firing anti-tank missiles and rockets into northern Israel in declared 'solidarity.' Israel responds with artillery and airstrikes. Both sides engage in daily limited exchanges that escalate gradually over 11 months. Over 60,000 Israelis are evacuated from the north; over 90,000 Lebanese are displaced from southern villages. Both sides suffer casualties in the sustained low-intensity conflict.

events.title: Pager & Walkie-Talkie Attacks Cripple Hezbollah
events.detail: On September 17–18, 2024, thousands of Hezbollah members' pagers and then walkie-talkies explode simultaneously across Lebanon and Syria in a sophisticated Israeli supply-chain infiltration operation. Explosives had been covertly embedded in devices during manufacture or transit. The attacks kill approximately 39 people and wound 3,000+, including Hezbollah fighters, their families, and bystanders. The operation decimates Hezbollah's secure communications infrastructure and is widely regarded as one of the most complex covert operations in modern intelligence history.

sources.name: NYT – How Israel Pulled Off the Pager Operation (2024)

events.title: Hassan Nasrallah Killed in Israeli Airstrike
events.detail: On September 27, 2024, Israeli Air Force F-35s drop 2,000-lb bunker-buster bombs on Hezbollah's underground command bunker in the Dahiyeh neighbourhood of south Beirut. Hezbollah Secretary-General Hassan Nasrallah — who had led the organisation since 1992, survived multiple Israeli assassination attempts, and become the most influential figure in Lebanese politics — is killed along with senior commanders. His death is the most significant blow to Hezbollah in its 40-year history.

sources.name: Al Jazeera – Hezbollah Confirms Nasrallah Death (Sep 28, 2024)

events.title: IDF Ground Operations in South Lebanon Begin
events.detail: On October 1, 2024, Israel launches ground operations into southern Lebanon with forces crossing the Blue Line. Targets include Hezbollah cross-border tunnel infrastructure, weapons caches, and command posts in border villages. Fighting is intense around Khiyam, Ayta ash-Sha'b, and Bint Jbeil. Massive Israeli airstrikes hit sites across Lebanon including Baalbek and Tripoli. By the time of the November 27 ceasefire, over 3,760 Lebanese have been killed in the September–November escalation period.

events.title: US-France Brokered Ceasefire Takes Effect Nov 27
events.detail: On November 27, 2024, a ceasefire agreement brokered by the United States and France takes effect, ending the escalated phase of the Lebanon-Israel conflict. Israel agrees to withdraw forces from Lebanese territory within 60 days; the Lebanese army is to deploy to the south; Hezbollah is to remain north of the Litani River under UNSCR 1701. A US-chaired monitoring committee oversees implementation. The ceasefire holds with minor violations; IDF withdrawal proceeds into early 2025, though Israel maintains positions in several strategic 'points' beyond the deadline.

sources.name: AFP – Lebanon-Israel Ceasefire Text and Implementation

events.title: Joseph Aoun Elected President — 791-Day Vacuum Ends
events.detail: On January 9, 2025, Lebanese Army Commander General Joseph Aoun is elected Lebanon's 14th president by parliament, ending a 791-day presidential vacancy (since Oct 31, 2022). Aoun, 60, maintained army neutrality during the 2024 conflict and coordinated post-ceasefire deployment to the south. He pledges to prioritise state sovereignty, disarmament under UNSCR 1701, IMF negotiations, and anti-corruption measures. His election follows US and Saudi diplomatic pressure on Lebanese parliamentarians.

sources.name: Reuters – Lebanon Elects Army Commander as President (Jan 2025)

events.title: Nawaf Salam Forms Reform Government
events.detail: In January 2025, President Aoun designates Nawaf Salam — former Lebanese ambassador to the UN (2007–2017) and former President of the International Court of Justice — as Prime Minister. Salam forms a reform-oriented cabinet in early February 2025, including technocratic ministers. Government priorities include IMF programme negotiations, overseeing IDF withdrawal and Lebanese army southern deployment, beginning reconstruction, and implementing structural economic reforms. Western capitals and Gulf states cautiously endorse the new leadership as Lebanon's best reform opportunity in a generation.

sources.name: Lebanese Prime Ministry – Nawaf Salam Government Formation (Feb 2025)
L'Orient Le Jour – Nawaf Salam Cabinet Profile (2025)

events.title: Post-War Reconstruction Begins — $8.5B Estimated Need
events.detail: The World Bank estimates reconstruction and recovery needs from the 2024 conflict at approximately $8.5 billion, on top of Lebanon's pre-existing financial crisis. International donors, Gulf states, and the EU pledge support conditional on governance reforms. The Lebanese army deploys across southern Lebanon in early 2025. IMF and Lebanon resume negotiations for a programme that had stalled since 2021. The Salam government submits a reform roadmap including central bank restructuring, public sector reform, and electricity sector overhaul.

sources.name: World Bank – Lebanon Recovery and Reconstruction Needs Assessment (2025)
